stem: Triangle \( DEF \) is similar to triangle \( ABC \), with vertices corresponding in the order \( A\leftrightarrow D \), \( B\leftrightarrow E \), \( C\leftrightarrow F \). In triangle \( ABC \), \( AB = [[a]] \) and \( BC = [[b]] \). If \( DE = [[d]] \), what is the length of \( EF \)?
solution: Corresponding sides of similar triangles are proportional, so \( \frac{EF}{BC} = \frac{DE}{AB} \). Thus \( EF = BC\cdot\frac{DE}{AB} = [[b]]\cdot\frac{[[d]]}{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio, using \( \frac{AB}{DE} \) instead of \( \frac{DE}{AB} \).
This multiplies by \( DE \) but forgets to divide by \( AB \).
This treats the sides as differing by a constant (additive) rather than a ratio.
This scales \( AB \) by \( DE \) instead of scaling \( BC \).
This is off from the correct length by a small amount.
This is off from the correct length by a small amount.

stem: In triangle \( ABC \), point \( D \) lies on \( AB \) and point \( E \) lies on \( AC \), with segment \( DE \) parallel to \( BC \). If \( AD = [[p]] \), \( DB = [[q]] \), and \( AE = [[r]] \), what is the length of \( EC \)?
solution: Since \( DE \parallel BC \), the Triangle Proportionality (Side-Splitter) Theorem gives \( \frac{AD}{DB} = \frac{AE}{EC} \). Solving for \( EC \): \( EC = AE\cdot\frac{DB}{AD} = [[r]]\cdot\frac{[[q]]}{[[p]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio \( \frac{DB}{AD} \) to \( \frac{AD}{DB} \).
This mixes up which segment plays the role of \( AE \) versus \( EC \) in the proportion.
This treats the segments as differing by a constant rather than in proportion.
This multiplies the two given segments on the wrong side of the triangle.
This is off from the correct length by one.
This is off from the correct length by one.

stem: Triangle \( ABC \) is similar to triangle \( DEF \), with corresponding sides \( AB \) and \( DE \) in the ratio \( AB : DE = [[a]] : [[b]] \). If the perimeter of triangle \( ABC \) is \( [[P]] \), what is the perimeter of triangle \( DEF \)?
solution: Perimeters of similar figures scale in the same ratio as their corresponding sides, so \( \frac{\text{perimeter}_{DEF}}{\text{perimeter}_{ABC}} = \frac{DE}{AB} = \frac{[[b]]}{[[a]]} \). Thus perimeter of \( DEF \) is \( [[P]]\cdot\frac{[[b]]}{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the side ratio.
This mistakenly squares the ratio, applying the area-scaling law to a perimeter (a length).
This treats the perimeters as differing additively rather than proportionally.
This multiplies by both ratio terms instead of just their ratio.
This is off from the correct perimeter by one.
This is off from the correct perimeter by one.

stem: Triangle \( ABC \) is similar to triangle \( DEF \), with corresponding sides \( AB \) and \( DE \) in the ratio \( AB : DE = [[a]] : [[b]] \). If the area of triangle \( ABC \) is \( [[A]] \), what is the area of triangle \( DEF \)?
solution: Areas of similar figures scale as the square of the ratio of corresponding sides, so \( \frac{\text{area}_{DEF}}{\text{area}_{ABC}} = \left(\frac{DE}{AB}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{[[b]]}{[[a]]}\right)^{2} \). Thus area of \( DEF \) is \( [[A]]\cdot\left(\frac{[[b]]}{[[a]]}\right)^{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This applies the perimeter (linear) scaling law instead of squaring the ratio.
This inverts the side ratio before squaring.
This treats the areas as differing additively rather than by a squared ratio.
This mistakenly cubes the ratio, applying the volume-scaling law to a plane figure.
This is off from the correct area by one.
This is off from the correct area by one.

stem: In right triangle \( ABC \), the altitude from the right angle to the hypotenuse divides the hypotenuse into two segments of length \( [[p]] \) and \( [[q]] \). What is the length of the altitude?
solution: The altitude to the hypotenuse creates two smaller triangles, each similar to the original; this gives the geometric-mean relation \( h = \sqrt{pq} = \sqrt{[[p]]\cdot [[q]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the arithmetic mean of the two segments instead of the geometric mean.
This multiplies the segments but forgets to take the square root.
This adds the segments before taking the square root instead of multiplying them.
This applies the Pythagorean theorem to the two segments, which is not the correct relationship here.
This is off from the correct altitude by one.
This is off from the correct altitude by one.

stem: In right triangle \( ABC \), the altitude from the right angle to the hypotenuse of length \( [[c]] \) meets the hypotenuse so that the segment adjacent to leg \( AB \) has length \( [[p]] \). What is the length of leg \( AB \)?
solution: The altitude to the hypotenuse makes each leg the geometric mean of the hypotenuse and its adjacent segment: \( AB^{2} = c\cdot p \), so \( AB = \sqrt{[[c]]\cdot [[p]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This subtracts the segment from the hypotenuse instead of multiplying, then takes a square root.
This adds the segment to the hypotenuse instead of multiplying, then takes a square root.
This multiplies the hypotenuse and segment but forgets to take the square root.
This applies the Pythagorean theorem to the hypotenuse and segment, which is not the correct relationship here.
This is off from the correct leg length by one.
This is off from the correct leg length by one.

stem: A vertical pole \( [[h1]] \) feet tall casts a shadow \( [[s1]] \) feet long. At the same moment, a nearby tree casts a shadow \( [[s2]] \) feet long. Assuming the pole, the tree, and their shadows form similar right triangles, what is the height of the tree, in feet?
solution: The pole's height-to-shadow ratio equals the tree's height-to-shadow ratio (similar triangles): \( \frac{h_1}{s_1} = \frac{h_2}{s_2} \). So the tree's height is \( h_2 = h_1\cdot\frac{s_2}{s_1} = [[h1]]\cdot\frac{[[s2]]}{[[s1]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the two shadow lengths in the ratio.
This divides by the pole's height instead of using it as a multiplier.
This treats the height and shadow lengths as differing additively rather than proportionally.
This multiplies by the tree's shadow but forgets to divide by the pole's shadow.
This is off from the correct height by one.
This is off from the correct height by one.
